result.question: Designing high-reliability LLM teacher labels for training a code-review judge model (WordPress/PHP quality scoring, 0-100 scale). Context: our stored teacher labels (single-pass, generated by an older Claude model) measured test-retest reliability ≈0.43 (Spearman triad method); today's Claude is ≈0.90 self-consistent on the same items; our fine-tuned student already saturates the noisy labels (ρ≈0.62 = ceiling). We will re-label ~700 train + ~120 val items. Research questions: (1) best practices for LLM-as-judge label quality — multi-sample aggregation (how many passes, mean vs median vs rank-average), rubric anchoring, pairwise-comparison labeling vs absolute scoring (which yields higher reliability for ordinal quality targets), panel-of-judges / multi-model ensembles, debate or critique-then-score protocols; (2) known results on label noise vs distillation/RL efficacy — how label reliability bounds student performance (attenuation), when re-labeling beats more data; (3) practical protocols used by frontier labs / open literature for reward-model or judge-model ground truth (e.g. RewardBench, JudgeBench, constitutional rubrics), especially for code quality; (4) pitfalls: score drift across batches, prompt-format sensitivity, position/verbosity bias in judge scoring, and mitigation. Deliverable: a concrete re-labeling protocol recommendation (passes, aggregation, rubric design, QC checks, sample-size math) we can execute with Claude Code agents at $0.
result.summary: To re-label the ~700 train + ~120 val items reliably, the evidence converges on a multi-sample, chance-corrected, bias-audited protocol rather than a single-pass judge. The single most important correction to the current plan: today's Claude being ~0.90 self-consistent is NOT evidence of valid labels — high test-retest reliability provably coexists with severe bias (the "consistency-bias paradox"), so QC must add chance-corrected agreement (Cohen's kappa / Krippendorff's alpha) and explicit bias audits, not just repeat-pass agreement. Because measurement error attenuates every downstream correlation (observed ρ ≈ ρ_true × √(rel_x·rel_y)), the student's ρ≈0.62 ceiling is largely a label-reliability artifact (√0.43 ≈ 0.66), so improving label reliability raises effective signal without adding data, and targeted active re-labeling of the noisiest items corrects labels up to 4× more efficiently than relabeling at random. For the 0–100 absolute-score target specifically, keep pointwise scoring (more robust to distractor/style manipulation: 9% flip vs 35% for pairwise) but stabilize it by averaging M independent passes (variance shrinks ~1/M), anchoring the rubric, using a reasoning/"thinking" judge that sees the full unstripped response, and — if you want to sharpen the ordinal ranking — layering a round-robin pairwise + Bradley-Terry pass to correct non-transitivity. Concretely: run 5+ independent temperature-0/low-temp passes per item with caching disabled, aggregate by median (or rank-average), report kappa/alpha as headline reliability over ≥3 replications, counterbalance any pairwise ordering (A-vs-B and B-vs-A), and spend extra passes on the items your correctness/difficulty estimator flags as noisiest.

findings.claim: Self-consistency does not imply valid labels: a judge can be near-perfectly test-retest reliable yet severely biased (the 'consistency-bias paradox'), and raw agreement overstates chance-corrected agreement by 33-41 points. QC must therefore report Cohen's kappa / Krippendorff's alpha as the headline metric, audit for bias when test-retest >0.95, and follow a Minimum Viable Validation Protocol: chance-corrected headline metric; position-bias check via paired AB+BA; test-retest over >=3 independent runs at temperature 0 with caching disabled; cross-validate on >=2 benchmarks spanning preference-style and correctness-style distributions.
findings.evidence: Qwen 3 8B: test-retest 0.992 but position bias 0.192; Gemini 2.5 Flash: 0.988 / 0.125. On MT-Bench a judge reporting '85% agreement' has kappa ~0.48 (33.8-41.3 pp deflation, cohort mean 38.6 pp). Directly refutes relying on the cited ~0.90 self-consistency figure as sufficient QC. Scope caveat: the paradox is demonstrated with position bias in pairwise setups; the reliability!=validity principle transfers to absolute scoring but position bias per se is a pairwise artifact.

findings.claim: Averaging judgments over an M-judge (or M-pass) panel reduces judge-driven variance components as ~1/M, materially improving reliability; on a safety benchmark averaging three judges cut the standard error by 38%. This is the mechanistic justification for multi-sample aggregation in the relabeling protocol.
findings.evidence: Both judge design-sensitivity (sigma^2_lambda) and item x judge interaction (sigma^2_alphaλ) shrink as 1/M when averaging over M judges (Eq. 3); a single judge inflates variance 40.6-106%. The exact 38% is specific to a judge-dominated safety benchmark and won't transfer numerically to code quality, but the 1/M shrinkage is textbook generalizability theory. Note: the related claim that judge choice is the single largest variance source (dominating prompt wording) was REFUTED 1-2, so do not over-rely on judge/model diversity over rubric quality.

findings.claim: For the 0-100 absolute-score target, keep pointwise scoring as the primary label because it is substantially more robust to spurious/distractor-feature manipulation and better reflects true quality; but pairwise comparison yields higher discrimination accuracy on coding tasks specifically, so an optional round-robin pairwise + Bradley-Terry pass can sharpen the ordinal ranking — provided non-transitivity and position bias are controlled.
findings.evidence: Pairwise preferences flip ~35% under distractor manipulation vs only 9% for absolute scores (2504.14716). CodeJudgeBench (2507.10535) finds pairwise outperforms scalar pointwise judging ON CODING accuracy. Tension is real and axis-dependent: absolute wins on distractor-robustness and yields the scalar scores you need; pairwise wins on discrimination/human-alignment but only yields rankings and needs an anchoring step. LLM judges have non-transitive preferences (rankings sensitive to baseline choice), but round-robin tournaments + Bradley-Terry raise Spearman vs Chatbot Arena 95.0->96.4% and Kendall 82.1->86.3% (clJIQ4TKR0). Cost caveat: round-robin is O(nm^2). A refuted claim (0-3) argued pairwise manufactures non-existent distinctions — so treat pairwise winners cautiously.

findings.claim: Label reliability imposes a hard ceiling on achievable student/judge fidelity: measurement error attenuates every observed correlation via the Spearman disattenuation formula (observed rho = true rho x sqrt(rel_x * rel_y)) and reduces statistical power, so noisy labels bound how high student-vs-teacher rho can go and also distort evaluation/model-selection metrics, not just training. Improving label reliability increases effective signal without adding data.
findings.evidence: Worked disattenuation example: observed rho^2=.10 with reliabilities .60/.70 -> true .238. Directly explains the project's numbers: sqrt(0.43)~0.66 vs observed ceiling ~0.62, i.e. the student already saturates the noisy labels. 'Label convergence' work shows contradictory annotations set an upper performance bound from annotation quality not model capacity (LVIS: SOTA 66.8 mAP sits inside the 62.63-67.52 convergence interval). Cross-domain (psychometrics + object detection + label-noise) but the attenuation principle is domain-general. Also: noisy labels confound measured metrics and rankings, so the val-set relabel matters as much as train.

findings.claim: The formal mechanism: naive training risk decomposes as E_x[(1-rho(x))L_clean + rho(x)L_noise], so where instance noise probability rho(x) is high the gradient is dominated by the erroneous signal, forcing the model to overfit corrupted labels. Single-pass LLM-as-judge labels are a documented, often large and highly variable, source of such noise and must be measured rather than assumed clean.
findings.evidence: Theorem 2.1 risk decomposition (2605.06036). Cleanlab-estimated noise ratios in RLHF preference datasets range 1.44% (PKU-SafeRLHF) to 47.77% (SHP), 38.97% (HH-RLHF) — large and variable. LLM-as-judge agents introduce systematic errors from hallucinations/capability deficits. Caveat: the two highest-noise datasets (SHP, HH-RLHF) are human-annotated, not LLM-judged; the operative conclusion (measure single-pass labels, don't assume clean) is sound. Scope: the decomposition is for naive training; robust losses can mitigate.

findings.claim: When budget is limited, targeted active re-labeling beats both random re-labeling and simply collecting more data: ranking items by estimated label correctness and labelling difficulty and re-annotating in that priority order corrects labels up to 4x more effectively than random selection under realistic noise.
findings.evidence: Active label cleaning (Nature Commun. 2022, Bernhardt et al.), validated on CIFAR-10H and a medical benchmark. For the protocol: spend extra passes / human-review budget on the highest-disagreement / hardest items rather than uniformly. Domain caveat: validated on image classification, not LLM-judge code labels; '4x' is a best-case ceiling depending on noise level and estimator quality.

findings.claim: Judge quality is improved by using a reasoning/'thinking' model that sees the full, unstripped response: retaining comments and reasoning in the judged response improves code-judge accuracy, and thinking models substantially outperform non-thinking ones on code judging — a small thinking model (Qwen3-8B) beat purpose-trained judges up to 70B, indicating reason-before-scoring matters more than size or judge-specific fine-tuning.
findings.vote: 3-0 on both, but generalization to absolute-ordinal scoring is inferred not tested
findings.evidence: CodeJudgeBench (2507.10535): stripping comments/reasoning degrades accuracy; thinking models significantly outperform non-thinking. Caveat: the ablation was run in a pairwise setting and 'reasoning' refers to reasoning inside the judged response — the critique-then-score / ordinal-scoring extension is a reasonable but untested inference. The same paper warns all models show 'significant randomness' in code judgment, reinforcing multi-sample aggregation.

findings.claim: Position and prompt-format bias are severe, concrete pitfalls in code judging and must be mitigated by order-randomization/counterbalancing: merely swapping response order can substantially change verdicts, and an injected sentiment/bias cue flipped Qwen2.5-Coder-3B on CodeRepair from 67.38% baseline to 92.96% (bias toward correct) or collapsed it to 12.66% (bias toward wrong) with code unchanged.
findings.evidence: CodeJudgeBench: changing presentation order substantially impacts pairwise accuracy. 'Bias in the Loop' (2604.16790): directional verdict reversal from injected cues on unchanged code. Mitigations: for any pairwise pass evaluate both AB and BA orderings and treat inconsistency as a tie; keep judge prompts free of leading/sentiment cues; fix a stable rubric-anchored prompt template to avoid format sensitivity. The 12.66%/92.96% figures are a single 3B model under the most extreme condition — scope, don't over-generalize the magnitude.

result.caveats: Source strength: the two most protocol-shaping papers (arXiv 2606.19544 'Reliability without Validity' and 2604.11581 'Hidden Measurement Error') are recent (2026) arXiv preprints, not peer-reviewed; their figures verified verbatim but the specific numbers (38% SE reduction, 33-41pp kappa deflation) are benchmark-specific and will not transfer numerically to a WordPress/PHP code-quality judge. Cross-domain transfer: the attenuation-ceiling and active-cleaning evidence (PMC5965627, label-convergence 2409.09412, Nature Commun. 2022) is strongest in psychometrics and image classification, not LLM ordinal code scoring — the principle is domain-general but the exact ceilings are analogical. The pairwise-vs-absolute question is genuinely contested (split 2-1 votes both directions, plus a 0-3 REFUTED claim that pairwise manufactures spurious distinctions): treat 'keep absolute as primary, pairwise as optional sharpener' as a defensible default, not a settled result. No source gives a validated passes-per-item number for absolute 0-100 scoring — the majority-vote convergence estimate (~11 trials) was REFUTED 0-3, so the '5+ passes' recommendation is an engineering default grounded in the 1/M variance argument, not an empirically derived optimum for this exact task. Finally, none of the sources address the $0/local-model execution constraint or PHP/WordPress code quality specifically; CodeJudgeBench is the closest (general coding tasks).
openQuestions: How many passes M is optimal for stable absolute 0-100 code-quality labels, and does mean, median, or rank-average aggregation minimize attenuation for this specific ordinal target? No source gives a validated number for pointwise scoring (the majority-vote ~11-trial estimate was refuted).

Does the pairwise>pointwise accuracy advantage on coding survive when the deliverable is a calibrated scalar 0-100 score rather than a ranking — i.e., is the round-robin+Bradley-Terry sharpening pass worth its O(nm^2) cost at $0/local-model budget?

What is the actual measured chance-corrected reliability (kappa/alpha) and position/verbosity bias of today's Claude on the specific WordPress/PHP rubric, and does it exhibit the consistency-bias paradox on this task?

Does critique-then-score (judge-produced critique before scoring), as opposed to merely retaining the response's own comments, improve absolute-ordinal code-quality reliability — this was inferred but not directly tested by any source?

refuted.claim: Judge/panel choice is the single largest source of variance in LLM evaluation, dominating prompt-wording effects: on a 141-item safety benchmark the judge panel contributed 43.8% of the variance of the aggregate score and item×judge interaction another 16.7%, while prompt wording contributed under 0.5%. This argues that for reliable ordinal quality labels, investing in judge/model diversity matters far more than rubric-prompt tweaking.

Aggregating repeated judge passes via majority vote recovers the stable (50-trial) verdict with 95% probability after ~11 trials on average, rising to ~15 for high-variance questions — giving a concrete passes-per-item target for multi-sample re-labeling.

Pairwise forced-choice labeling is noisier and can manufacture ordinal distinctions that absolute pointwise scoring shows are non-existent: pairwise verdicts flip 13.6% of the time on average while mean pointwise score gaps (0.19-0.36 on a 10-point scale) are not statistically significant, so a pairwise 'winner' does not imply a real quality difference.
